topic.what: Comprehensive review of Triphala — the classic Ayurvedic rasayana formula comprising Amla (Emblica officinalis), Haritaki (Terminalia chebula), and Bibhitaki (Terminalia bellerica) — covering its phytochemistry, pharmacology, and clinical evidence.
topic.how: Systematic literature survey synthesizing in-vitro, in-vivo, and clinical studies; examines mechanisms including anti-inflammatory cytokine suppression, antioxidant enzyme induction, modulation of gut microbiota, and mucosal cytoprotection.
topic.why_matters: Triphala is the most widely studied Ayurvedic polyherbal formula and represents a model for synergistic multi-herb pharmacology; its prebiotic and gastroprotective properties offer mechanistic bridges to modern gastroenterology and microbiome science.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Reduction in gastric ulcer index and mucosal lesion scores across reviewed animal studies
characteristics.value: Consistent protective effect across multiple animal models
characteristics.evidence: Multiple in-vivo studies cited in the review showing significant ulcer index reduction
characteristics.context: Animal models; limited high-quality RCT data in humans

characteristics.what_it_measures: Shift in gut microbial composition toward beneficial taxa (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium)
characteristics.value: Promotes growth of beneficial bacteria; inhibits pathogens in vitro
characteristics.evidence: In-vitro fermentation studies and limited in-vivo rodent data reviewed
characteristics.context: Mostly preclinical; human microbiome intervention studies lacking

characteristics.what_it_measures: Inhibition of NF-kB pathway and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β)
characteristics.value: Significant downregulation in cell-line and animal studies
characteristics.evidence: Multiple mechanistic studies reviewed; consistent across component herbs
characteristics.context: Preclinical; mechanism well-characterised but clinical translation not confirmed

characteristics.what_it_measures: DPPH radical scavenging, superoxide dismutase and catalase induction
characteristics.value: High polyphenol content (gallic acid, ellagic acid, chebulinic acid) confers strong antioxidant activity
characteristics.vs_baseline: Ascorbic acid standard in DPPH assays
characteristics.evidence: Consistently reported across reviewed phytochemical studies
characteristics.context: In-vitro antioxidant assays; in-vivo oxidative stress markers also supportive

good_for: Understanding polyherbal synergy as a mechanistic framework
Bridging Ayurvedic rasayana theory to microbiome and mucosal defense science
Identifying candidate compounds (gallic acid, ellagic acid) for further isolation studies
Informing combination-herb strategies for gastric inflammation and dysbiosis
not_for: Definitive clinical guidance — insufficient high-quality RCT evidence
H. pylori eradication — no direct antimicrobial eradication evidence reviewed
requires: Cross-referencing with primary pharmacology studies for mechanism depth
Clinical validation studies before therapeutic claims
Standardisation data on active constituent concentrations

novelty: Most comprehensive single review of Triphala across gastroprotection, prebiotics, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory dimensions as of 2021
Explicitly frames Triphala as a prebiotic agent — novel framing bridging classical rasayana theory and modern microbiome science
Highlights polyherbal synergy: combined formula outperforms individual components in several assays, supporting the classical formulation rationale
open_problems: No adequately powered RCTs for gastritis or functional dyspepsia endpoints
Standardisation and batch-to-batch variability of active constituents unresolved
Mechanism of prebiotic action (specific metabolites, receptor targets) incompletely characterised
Interaction with H. pylori infection not addressed
Pharmacokinetic data in humans (bioavailability of ellagitannins, conversion to urolithin A) not synthesised
